    Copied! 
    from pyhydra.climate.spatial_analysis import (
    fit_gev_mle,
    fit_gev_lmom,
    fit_gev_bayes,
    return_level,
    return_level_bayes,
    regional_index_flood,
    fit_regional_gev,
    regional_return_levels,
    regional_discordancy,
    regional_heterogeneity,
)

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    from pyhydra.climate.spatial_analysis import ( fit_gev_mle, fit_gev_lmom, fit_gev_bayes, return_level, return_level_bayes, regional_index_flood, fit_regional_gev, regional_return_levels, regional_discordancy, regional_heterogeneity, ) import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

    Regional Frequency Analysis (GEV)¶

    What is Regional Frequency Analysis?¶

    At-site frequency analysis fits a distribution to the record at a single gauge. For rare events (T > 50 yr), the uncertainty is often so large that the result is almost useless — a 40-year record can only directly observe ~40 data points, barely enough to fit a 3-parameter distribution.

    Regional Frequency Analysis (RFA) solves this by pooling data from multiple stations in a hydrologically homogeneous region. The key insight: extreme event statistics (especially the shape parameter ξ) are similar across a region even when mean flows differ. By normalising each station, we can pool perhaps 200–300 station-years into a single regional estimate.

    The Index Flood method (Dalrymple 1960)¶

    1. Index flood = each station's mean annual maximum (Q̄_s)
    2. Normalise: divide each station's series by its own index flood → dimensionless "growth curve" series
    3. Pool: concatenate all normalised series (now comparable between stations)
    4. Fit: estimate one regional GEV on the pooled data
    5. Rescale: multiply regional T-year quantile by each station's index flood to get site-specific return levels

    The fundamental assumption: all stations in the region share the same dimensionless GEV (same ξ, same shape, different means).

    Fitting methods available¶

    Function Method Dependencies Recommended for
    fit_gev_mle Maximum Likelihood scipy Samples ≥ 30
    fit_gev_lmom L-moments lmoments3 Any sample size, robust to outliers
    fit_gev_bayes Bayesian MCMC (PyMC) pymc Full uncertainty quantification
    return_level_bayes Posterior return levels pymc Credible intervals on return levels

    Installation¶

    pip install scipy lmoments3    # core methods
pip install pymc               # Bayesian method only

    Synthetic dataset¶

    Eight stations representing major Iberian river basins, generated with:

    • Shared shape parameter ξ ≈ 0.12–0.20: homogeneous region assumption
    • Different location/scale: reflecting the index-flood structure (mean annual maximum varies by basin)
    • Different record lengths (25–45 years): realistic heterogeneity

    The synthetic data allows us to know the "true" parameters and validate the regional estimates.

    In a real study, the homogeneity assumption should be tested before pooling — see the Hosking & Wallis heterogeneity check in Section 3.

    In [3]:
    Copied! 
    from scipy.stats import genextreme

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# Station characteristics
stations = {
    "Ebro":     {"mu": 1200, "sigma": 350, "xi": 0.15, "n": 40},
    "Tajo":     {"mu":  900, "sigma": 260, "xi": 0.12, "n": 35},
    "Duero":    {"mu": 1100, "sigma": 310, "xi": 0.14, "n": 45},
    "Guadalquivir": {"mu": 800, "sigma": 240, "xi": 0.10, "n": 38},
    "Turia":    {"mu":  350, "sigma": 120, "xi": 0.18, "n": 30},
    "Segura":   {"mu":  280, "sigma":  90, "xi": 0.20, "n": 25},
    "Miño":     {"mu":  700, "sigma": 200, "xi": 0.08, "n": 42},
    "Jucar":    {"mu":  420, "sigma": 130, "xi": 0.16, "n": 33},
}

am_data = {}
for name, p in stations.items():
    # genextreme uses -xi convention in scipy
    am_data[name] = genextreme.rvs(-p["xi"], loc=p["mu"], scale=p["sigma"],
                                    size=p["n"], random_state=rng)

# Quick overview
summary = pd.DataFrame({
    name: {"n": len(v), "mean": v.mean(), "std": v.std(),
           "max": v.max(), "min": v.min()}
    for name, v in am_data.items()
}).T.round(0)
print(summary)
    from scipy.stats import genextreme rng = np.random.default_rng(42) # Station characteristics stations = { "Ebro": {"mu": 1200, "sigma": 350, "xi": 0.15, "n": 40}, "Tajo": {"mu": 900, "sigma": 260, "xi": 0.12, "n": 35}, "Duero": {"mu": 1100, "sigma": 310, "xi": 0.14, "n": 45}, "Guadalquivir": {"mu": 800, "sigma": 240, "xi": 0.10, "n": 38}, "Turia": {"mu": 350, "sigma": 120, "xi": 0.18, "n": 30}, "Segura": {"mu": 280, "sigma": 90, "xi": 0.20, "n": 25}, "Miño": {"mu": 700, "sigma": 200, "xi": 0.08, "n": 42}, "Jucar": {"mu": 420, "sigma": 130, "xi": 0.16, "n": 33}, } am_data = {} for name, p in stations.items(): # genextreme uses -xi convention in scipy am_data[name] = genextreme.rvs(-p["xi"], loc=p["mu"], scale=p["sigma"], size=p["n"], random_state=rng) # Quick overview summary = pd.DataFrame({ name: {"n": len(v), "mean": v.mean(), "std": v.std(), "max": v.max(), "min": v.min()} for name, v in am_data.items() }).T.round(0) print(summary)
                     n    mean    std     max    min
Ebro          40.0  1504.0  516.0  2932.0  833.0
Tajo          35.0  1003.0  238.0  1436.0  523.0
Duero         45.0  1280.0  380.0  2482.0  724.0
Guadalquivir  38.0   895.0  281.0  1693.0  498.0
Turia         30.0   482.0  224.0  1286.0  221.0
Segura        25.0   329.0  134.0   746.0  167.0
Miño          42.0   844.0  252.0  1474.0  436.0
Jucar         33.0   436.0  143.0   819.0  262.0

    1. At-site fitting¶

    Three estimation methods applied to a single station. In practice, use all three:

    • L-moments: fast, always converges, recommended starting point
    • MLE: more efficient (lower variance) for n ≥ 30, needed for likelihood-ratio tests
    • Bayesian MCMC: full posterior; use for short records or when uncertainty quantification is critical

    The comparison across methods reveals estimator stability — large disagreement between MLE and L-moments signals a problematic sample (outliers, poor convergence).

    In [4]:
    Copied! 
    # Pick one station for the at-site demo
station_name = "Ebro"
data = am_data[station_name]

print(f"Station: {station_name}  |  n = {len(data)}  |  mean = {data.mean():.0f} m³/s")
    # Pick one station for the at-site demo station_name = "Ebro" data = am_data[station_name] print(f"Station: {station_name} | n = {len(data)} | mean = {data.mean():.0f} m³/s")
    Station: Ebro  |  n = 40  |  mean = 1504 m³/s
    In [5]:
    Copied! 
    # --- MLE ---
params_mle = fit_gev_mle(data)
print("MLE parameters:")
print(f"  mu = {params_mle['mu']:.1f}  sigma = {params_mle['sigma']:.1f}  xi = {params_mle['xi']:.3f}")
    # --- MLE --- params_mle = fit_gev_mle(data) print("MLE parameters:") print(f" mu = {params_mle['mu']:.1f} sigma = {params_mle['sigma']:.1f} xi = {params_mle['xi']:.3f}")
    MLE parameters:
  mu = 1244.6  sigma = 341.9  xi = 0.168
    In [6]:
    Copied! 
    # --- L-moments (requires lmoments3) ---
try:
    params_lmom = fit_gev_lmom(data)
    print("L-moments parameters:")
    print(f"  mu = {params_lmom['mu']:.1f}  sigma = {params_lmom['sigma']:.1f}  xi = {params_lmom['xi']:.3f}")
except ImportError:
    params_lmom = params_mle   # fallback for the rest of the notebook
    print("(lmoments3 not installed — pip install lmoments3)")
    # --- L-moments (requires lmoments3) --- try: params_lmom = fit_gev_lmom(data) print("L-moments parameters:") print(f" mu = {params_lmom['mu']:.1f} sigma = {params_lmom['sigma']:.1f} xi = {params_lmom['xi']:.3f}") except ImportError: params_lmom = params_mle # fallback for the rest of the notebook print("(lmoments3 not installed — pip install lmoments3)")
    L-moments parameters:
  mu = 1250.4  sigma = 367.7  xi = 0.103
    In [7]:
    Copied! 
    # Bayesian MCMC — 2 chains / 500 samples for speed (use 4 / 1000 for production)
posterior = fit_gev_bayes(data, n_chains=2, n_samples=500)
print("Bayesian GEV posterior (Ebro station):")
print(posterior.describe().round(2))
    # Bayesian MCMC — 2 chains / 500 samples for speed (use 4 / 1000 for production) posterior = fit_gev_bayes(data, n_chains=2, n_samples=500) print("Bayesian GEV posterior (Ebro station):") print(posterior.describe().round(2)) 
    Initializing NUTS using jitter+adapt_diag...
Multiprocess sampling (2 chains in 2 jobs)
NUTS: [mu_raw, sigma, xi]

    Output()
    

    

    

    

    

    

    Sampling 2 chains for 1_000 tune and 500 draw iterations (2_000 + 1_000 draws total) took 0 seconds.
We recommend running at least 4 chains for robust computation of convergence diagnostics

std      67.36    57.43     0.15

    Return level plot¶
    The return level plot (Gumbel probability paper) shows:
    

      

    • Empirical points (Gringorten plotting positions): observed data without any model assumption
      • 

    • Fitted curves: model extrapolation into unobserved return periods
      • 

    • Uncertainty bands: widen rapidly for T > record length — this is expected
      • 

    

    

    Reading the plot: Where the fitted curve passes through the empirical cloud is well-constrained. Where it extrapolates beyond the rightmost point is model-dependent — this is where different methods (MLE vs Bayes) differ most.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    In [8]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    T_values = [2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000]

rl_mle  = [return_level(params_mle,  T) for T in T_values]
rl_lmom = [return_level(params_lmom, T) for T in T_values]

# Empirical plotting position (Gringorten)
data_sorted = np.sort(data)
n = len(data_sorted)
prob = (np.arange(1, n + 1) - 0.44) / (n + 0.12)
T_emp = 1.0 / (1.0 - prob)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.semilogx(T_emp, data_sorted, 'ko', ms=4, label="Observed", zorder=4)
ax.semilogx(T_values, rl_mle,  'b-',  lw=2, label="GEV MLE")
ax.semilogx(T_values, rl_lmom, 'r--', lw=2, label="GEV L-moments")
ax.set_xlabel("Return period (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Annual maximum discharge (m³/s)")
ax.set_title(f"Return level plot — {station_name}", fontsize=12)
ax.legend()
ax.grid(which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Table
rl_df = pd.DataFrame({"T": T_values, "MLE": np.round(rl_mle, 0), "L-mom": np.round(rl_lmom, 0)})
rl_df.set_index("T", inplace=True)
rl_df

    

    T_values = [2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000]

rl_mle  = [return_level(params_mle,  T) for T in T_values]
rl_lmom = [return_level(params_lmom, T) for T in T_values]

# Empirical plotting position (Gringorten)
data_sorted = np.sort(data)
n = len(data_sorted)
prob = (np.arange(1, n + 1) - 0.44) / (n + 0.12)
T_emp = 1.0 / (1.0 - prob)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.semilogx(T_emp, data_sorted, 'ko', ms=4, label="Observed", zorder=4)
ax.semilogx(T_values, rl_mle,  'b-',  lw=2, label="GEV MLE")
ax.semilogx(T_values, rl_lmom, 'r--', lw=2, label="GEV L-moments")
ax.set_xlabel("Return period (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Annual maximum discharge (m³/s)")
ax.set_title(f"Return level plot — {station_name}", fontsize=12)
ax.legend()
ax.grid(which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Table
rl_df = pd.DataFrame({"T": T_values, "MLE": np.round(rl_mle, 0), "L-mom": np.round(rl_lmom, 0)})
rl_df.set_index("T", inplace=True)
rl_df

    Bayesian return levels with credible intervals¶
    When fit_gev_bayes is available, return_level_bayes extracts posterior
quantiles to build a credible interval around the return level curve.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    In [9]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.semilogx(T_emp, data_sorted, "ko", ms=4, label="Observed", zorder=4)

# Return periods below 2 years are not useful for annual-maxima analysis
# (T=1 → p=0 hits the distribution boundary and produces extreme/non-finite values).
T_fine = np.logspace(np.log10(2), 3, 80)   # T from 2 to 1000 years

medians, lowers, uppers = [], [], []
for T in T_fine:
    ci = return_level_bayes(posterior, T, credible=0.90)
    medians.append(ci["median"])
    lowers.append(ci["lower"])
    uppers.append(ci["upper"])

ax.fill_between(T_fine, lowers, uppers, alpha=0.25, color="steelblue", label="90% CI")
ax.semilogx(T_fine, medians, "b-", lw=2, label="Posterior median")
ax.set_xlabel("Return period (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Annual maximum discharge (m³/s)")
ax.set_title(f"Bayesian GEV — station {station_name}", fontsize=11)
ax.legend()
ax.grid(which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

    

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.semilogx(T_emp, data_sorted, "ko", ms=4, label="Observed", zorder=4)

# Return periods below 2 years are not useful for annual-maxima analysis
# (T=1 → p=0 hits the distribution boundary and produces extreme/non-finite values).
T_fine = np.logspace(np.log10(2), 3, 80)   # T from 2 to 1000 years

medians, lowers, uppers = [], [], []
for T in T_fine:
    ci = return_level_bayes(posterior, T, credible=0.90)
    medians.append(ci["median"])
    lowers.append(ci["lower"])
    uppers.append(ci["upper"])

ax.fill_between(T_fine, lowers, uppers, alpha=0.25, color="steelblue", label="90% CI")
ax.semilogx(T_fine, medians, "b-", lw=2, label="Posterior median")
ax.set_xlabel("Return period (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Annual maximum discharge (m³/s)")
ax.set_title(f"Bayesian GEV — station {station_name}", fontsize=11)
ax.legend()
ax.grid(which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    


    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    2. Regional frequency analysis — Index Flood method¶
    Assumptions and pre-conditions¶
    Before pooling, verify:
    

      

    1. Hydrological homogeneity: stations should have similar flood generation mechanisms (same climate zone, similar basin characteristics)
      2. 

    2. Discordancy: no single station should have unusual statistics that distort the regional fit
      3. 

    3. Record independence: station records should not overlap in time (avoid double-counting shared events — check cross-correlations)
      4. 

    

    Pooling mechanism¶
    After normalising (dividing by the index flood), all station series should have mean ≈ 1.0. Fitting one GEV to the pooled data effectively uses sum(n_i) station-years instead of n_i from a single station — potentially a 5–10× increase in effective sample size.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    In [10]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    _, index_floods = regional_index_flood(am_data)
print("Index floods (mean annual maximum, m³/s):")
for name, val in index_floods.items():
    print(f"  {name:<15}: {val:.0f}")

    

    _, index_floods = regional_index_flood(am_data)
print("Index floods (mean annual maximum, m³/s):")
for name, val in index_floods.items():
    print(f"  {name:<15}: {val:.0f}")

    # Fit regional GEV
regional_params, _ = fit_regional_gev(am_data, method="lmom")   # or 'mle'
print("\nRegional GEV parameters (L-moments):")
print(f"  mu = {regional_params['mu']:.3f}  sigma = {regional_params['sigma']:.3f}  xi = {regional_params['xi']:.3f}")
print("  (fitted to standardised series — all means ≈ 1.0)")

    

    # Fit regional GEV
regional_params, _ = fit_regional_gev(am_data, method="lmom")   # or 'mle'
print("\nRegional GEV parameters (L-moments):")
print(f"  mu = {regional_params['mu']:.3f}  sigma = {regional_params['sigma']:.3f}  xi = {regional_params['xi']:.3f}")
print("  (fitted to standardised series — all means ≈ 1.0)")

    # Regional return levels at all stations

rl_regional = regional_return_levels(am_data, T_values=T_values, method="lmom")
print("Regional return levels (m³/s):")
rl_regional.round(0)

    

    # Regional return levels at all stations
T_values = [2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500]
rl_regional = regional_return_levels(am_data, T_values=T_values, method="lmom")
print("Regional return levels (m³/s):")
rl_regional.round(0)

    Regional GEV with uncertainty — Bayesian pooling¶
    fit_regional_gev/regional_return_levels also accept method='bayes':
the pooled, index-flood-normalised data are fit with fit_gev_bayes (PyMC
NUTS), giving a posterior over the regional dimensionless GEV instead of a
single point estimate. Each station's posterior return level is that
regional posterior scaled by its own index flood, so regional_return_levels
returns three DataFrames — (median, lower, upper) — instead of one.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    In [13]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    station_names = list(am_data.keys())

# Bayesian regional fit — 2 chains / 500 samples for speed (use 4 / 1000 for production)
rl_median, rl_lower, rl_upper = regional_return_levels(
    am_data, T_values=T_values, method="bayes", credible=0.90, n_chains=2, n_samples=500,
)

station_demo = station_names[0]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.fill_between(
    T_values, rl_lower.loc[station_demo], rl_upper.loc[station_demo],
    alpha=0.25, color="steelblue", label="90% CI (regional Bayes)",
)
ax.plot(T_values, rl_median.loc[station_demo], "b-", lw=2, label="Posterior median (regional Bayes)")
ax.plot(T_values, rl_regional.loc[station_demo], "r--", lw=2, label="Regional L-moments")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Return period (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Discharge (m³/s)")
ax.set_title(f"Regional return levels with uncertainty — {station_demo}", fontsize=11)
ax.legend()
ax.grid(which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

rl_median.round(0)

    

    station_names = list(am_data.keys())

# Bayesian regional fit — 2 chains / 500 samples for speed (use 4 / 1000 for production)
rl_median, rl_lower, rl_upper = regional_return_levels(
    am_data, T_values=T_values, method="bayes", credible=0.90, n_chains=2, n_samples=500,
)

station_demo = station_names[0]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
ax.fill_between(
    T_values, rl_lower.loc[station_demo], rl_upper.loc[station_demo],
    alpha=0.25, color="steelblue", label="90% CI (regional Bayes)",
)
ax.plot(T_values, rl_median.loc[station_demo], "b-", lw=2, label="Posterior median (regional Bayes)")
ax.plot(T_values, rl_regional.loc[station_demo], "r--", lw=2, label="Regional L-moments")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Return period (years)")
ax.set_ylabel("Discharge (m³/s)")
ax.set_title(f"Regional return levels with uncertainty — {station_demo}", fontsize=11)
ax.legend()
ax.grid(which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

rl_median.round(0)

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    Initializing NUTS using jitter+adapt_diag...
Multiprocess sampling (2 chains in 2 jobs)
NUTS: [mu_raw, sigma, xi]

    

    

    

    

    

    

    Output()
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    Sampling 2 chains for 1_000 tune and 500 draw iterations (2_000 + 1_000 draws total) took 0 seconds.
We recommend running at least 4 chains for robust computation of convergence diagnostics

    In [14]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    # Compare at-site MLE vs regional L-mom for each station
station_names = list(am_data.keys())
T_ref = [10, 100]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
for ax, T in zip(axes, T_ref):
    atsite = [
        return_level(fit_gev_mle(am_data[n]), T)
        for n in station_names
    ]
    regional = rl_regional[f"T{T}"].values

    x = np.arange(len(station_names))
    ax.bar(x - 0.2, atsite,   width=0.35, label="At-site MLE",    color="steelblue", alpha=0.8)
    ax.bar(x + 0.2, regional, width=0.35, label="Regional L-mom", color="tomato",    alpha=0.8)
    ax.set_xticks(x)
    ax.set_xticklabels(station_names, rotation=35, ha="right", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylabel("Discharge (m³/s)")
    ax.set_title(f"T = {T} years", fontsize=11)
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

plt.suptitle("At-site MLE vs regional L-moments — return levels", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

    

    # Compare at-site MLE vs regional L-mom for each station
station_names = list(am_data.keys())
T_ref = [10, 100]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
for ax, T in zip(axes, T_ref):
    atsite = [
        return_level(fit_gev_mle(am_data[n]), T)
        for n in station_names
    ]
    regional = rl_regional[f"T{T}"].values

    x = np.arange(len(station_names))
    ax.bar(x - 0.2, atsite,   width=0.35, label="At-site MLE",    color="steelblue", alpha=0.8)
    ax.bar(x + 0.2, regional, width=0.35, label="Regional L-mom", color="tomato",    alpha=0.8)
    ax.set_xticks(x)
    ax.set_xticklabels(station_names, rotation=35, ha="right", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylabel("Discharge (m³/s)")
    ax.set_title(f"T = {T} years", fontsize=11)
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

plt.suptitle("At-site MLE vs regional L-moments — return levels", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    


    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    Interpreting at-site vs regional disagreements¶
    When at-site MLE and the regional estimate differ substantially, use the following
decision tree to determine which to trust:
    

    


    
Scenario
What you see
Recommended estimate

    
    


    
Good record (n ≥ 30), homogeneous region
At-site ≈ regional (< 20% difference)
Average the two; report both

    

    
Short record (n < 20), at-site >> regional
At-site inflated by one extreme outlier
Regional — more stable

    

    
Short record (n < 20), at-site << regional
Station missed the large events
Regional with caution

    

    
Long record (n > 40), at-site >> regional
True local hydraulic behaviour
At-site — station has a different regime

    

    
Station is a hydraulic outlier (e.g., karst, regulated)
Very different L-CV
Exclude from region — fit at-site only

    
    

    

    Rule of thumb: The benefit of regionalisation decreases as at-site record length
increases. For n > 50 years, at-site estimates are usually as reliable as regional
ones (sampling variance becomes acceptable).
    

    Leave-one-out (LOO) cross-validation¶
    The most rigorous validation of the regional model is LOO cross-validation:
    

      

    1. Remove one station at a time from the pool
      2. 

    2. Refit the regional GEV with the remaining stations
      3. 

    3. Predict the return level at the removed station using its index flood
      4. 

    4. Compare against its at-site estimate
      5. 

    

    # LOO cross-validation (not implemented in pyhydra — example code)
from sklearn.model_selection import LeaveOneOut
station_list = list(am_data.keys())

for val_station in station_list:
    # Pool without the validation station
    train_data = {k: v for k, v in am_data.items() if k != val_station}
    train_params, train_idx = fit_regional_gev(train_data, method='lmom')
    
    # Index flood at the validation station
    idx_val = am_data[val_station].mean()
    
    # Regional prediction for the validation station
    T_cv = 100
    regional_rl = return_level(train_params, T_cv) * idx_val
    
    # At-site estimate at the validation station
    atsite_rl = return_level(fit_gev_mle(am_data[val_station]), T_cv)
    
    print(f'{val_station}: regional={regional_rl:.0f}  at-site={atsite_rl:.0f}')

    

    A well-validated regional model produces LOO predictions within ±30% of at-site
estimates for most stations. Large errors at a specific station are a signal that
it should be removed from the regional pool.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    3. Hosking & Wallis (1997) homogeneity diagnostics¶
    Two complementary statistics test whether pooling is appropriate — both
implemented in pyhydra.climate.spatial_analysis.rfa, with formulas
cross-checked against Hosking & Wallis's own reference implementation
(lmomRFA::regtst.s() in the R package lmomRFA).
    

    Discordancy (Di) — is any single station unusual?¶
    regional_discordancy computes, for each station, a Mahalanobis-type
distance between that station's (L-CV, L-skew, L-kurtosis) and the
(unweighted) regional average. A station with Di above its region-size
critical value (Hosking & Wallis 1997, Table 3.1) is a likely data error or a
genuinely different regime — investigate before pooling.
    

    Heterogeneity (H) — is the region internally consistent?¶
    regional_heterogeneity compares the observed, record-length-weighted
dispersion of at-site L-CV to the dispersion expected from Monte Carlo
simulations of a homogeneous region (drawn from a kappa distribution fitted
to the regional-average L-moments).
    

    Interpretation (Hosking & Wallis 1997):
    

      

    • H < 1: acceptably homogeneous — proceed with pooling
      • 

    • 1 ≤ H < 2: possibly heterogeneous — pool cautiously, check discordant stations
      • 

    • H ≥ 2: definitely heterogeneous — do not pool as a single region
      • 

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    In [15]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    try:
    disc = regional_discordancy(am_data)
    print("Discordancy statistic Di (Hosking & Wallis 1997, Table 3.1):")
    print(disc.round(3))
    if disc["discordant"].any():
        print(f"\n→ Discordant station(s): {disc.index[disc['discordant']].tolist()}")
    else:
        print("\n→ No station exceeds its critical Di — no discordant stations.")

    het = regional_heterogeneity(am_data, n_sim=500, seed=42)
    print(f"\nHeterogeneity statistic H1 = {het['H']:.2f}  "
          f"(V_obs={het['V_obs']:.4f}, simulated null: {het['V_sim_mean']:.4f} ± {het['V_sim_std']:.4f})")
    if het["H"] < 1:
        print("→ H < 1: acceptably homogeneous — proceed with pooling")
    elif het["H"] < 2:
        print("→ 1 ≤ H < 2: possibly heterogeneous — pool cautiously, check discordant stations")
    else:
        print("→ H ≥ 2: definitely heterogeneous — do not pool as a single region")

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3))
    disc["Di"].plot(kind="bar", ax=ax, color="steelblue", alpha=0.8)
    ax.axhline(disc["critical"].iloc[0], color="red", lw=1.5, ls="--", label="Critical Di")
    ax.set_ylabel("Di")
    ax.set_title(f"Discordancy by station (H1 = {het['H']:.2f})", fontsize=11)
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    plt.xticks(rotation=30)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

except ImportError:
    print("(lmoments3 not installed — pip install lmoments3)")

    

    try:
    disc = regional_discordancy(am_data)
    print("Discordancy statistic Di (Hosking & Wallis 1997, Table 3.1):")
    print(disc.round(3))
    if disc["discordant"].any():
        print(f"\n→ Discordant station(s): {disc.index[disc['discordant']].tolist()}")
    else:
        print("\n→ No station exceeds its critical Di — no discordant stations.")

    het = regional_heterogeneity(am_data, n_sim=500, seed=42)
    print(f"\nHeterogeneity statistic H1 = {het['H']:.2f}  "
          f"(V_obs={het['V_obs']:.4f}, simulated null: {het['V_sim_mean']:.4f} ± {het['V_sim_std']:.4f})")
    if het["H"] < 1:
        print("→ H < 1: acceptably homogeneous — proceed with pooling")
    elif het["H"] < 2:
        print("→ 1 ≤ H < 2: possibly heterogeneous — pool cautiously, check discordant stations")
    else:
        print("→ H ≥ 2: definitely heterogeneous — do not pool as a single region")

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3))
    disc["Di"].plot(kind="bar", ax=ax, color="steelblue", alpha=0.8)
    ax.axhline(disc["critical"].iloc[0], color="red", lw=1.5, ls="--", label="Critical Di")
    ax.set_ylabel("Di")
    ax.set_title(f"Discordancy by station (H1 = {het['H']:.2f})", fontsize=11)
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    plt.xticks(rotation=30)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

except ImportError:
    print("(lmoments3 not installed — pip install lmoments3)")

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    Discordancy statistic Di (Hosking & Wallis 1997, Table 3.1):
                 Di  critical  discordant
Ebro          0.026      2.14       False
Tajo          1.780      2.14       False
Duero         0.598      2.14       False
Guadalquivir  1.587      2.14       False
Turia         1.924      2.14       False
Segura        0.682      2.14       False
Miño          0.147      2.14       False
Jucar         1.255      2.14       False

→ No station exceeds its critical Di — no discordant stations.

Heterogeneity statistic H1 = 1.86  (V_obs=0.0300, simulated null: 0.0201 ± 0.0053)
→ 1 ≤ H < 2: possibly heterogeneous — pool cautiously, check discordant stations

    

    

    

    

    

    


    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    What to do when heterogeneity tests fail¶
    A non-homogeneous H in this demo is partly by design (different σ/μ ratios
across basins) and partly because the synthetic dataset doesn't enforce strict
homogeneity in L-CV. In real studies, several strategies are available:
    

    


    
Approach
When to use
How

    
    


    
Remove discordant stations
One station has clearly different L-CV
Re-run heterogeneity test without the outlier

    

    
Split into sub-regions
Clear geographic or climatic boundary
e.g. Mediterranean vs Atlantic; north vs south

    

    
Hierarchical Bayes
Heterogeneous but related region
See bayes_hierarchical notebook — partial pooling adapts to varying L-CV

    

    
Weighted pooling
L-CV varies smoothly
Weight each station by 1/L-CV² when fitting the regional distribution

    

    
Accept heterogeneity
n is large (> 40) at most stations
At-site estimates are reliable enough; use regional only for short-record stations

    
    

    

    

    Hosking & Wallis (1997) criterion: regional_heterogeneity (Section 3
above) implements the formal H-statistic — Monte Carlo simulation from a
kappa distribution assesses whether the observed L-CV spread is consistent
with random sampling from a homogeneous region.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    4. Flood frequency map¶
    Interpolate regional return levels onto a spatial grid using IDW to produce a
flood frequency map — combines regional_return_levels with
IDWInterpolator.
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    In [16]:
    

    

    


    
Copied!


    
    

    

    from pyhydra.climate.spatial_analysis import IDWInterpolator

# Approximate station coordinates (Iberian Peninsula)
station_coords = pd.DataFrame({
    "lon": [-0.88, -5.63, -5.10, -5.60, -0.39, -1.05, -7.86, -1.16],
    "lat": [41.66, 39.68, 41.55, 37.95, 39.46, 38.11, 42.60, 39.17],
}, index=station_names)

# Grid for Spain
glon2 = np.arange(-9.5, 4.5, 0.3)
glat2 = np.arange(35.5, 44.5, 0.3)
GLON2, GLAT2 = np.meshgrid(glon2, glat2)
grid2 = pd.DataFrame({"lon": GLON2.ravel(), "lat": GLAT2.ravel()})

T_map = 100
station_coords[f"T{T_map}"] = rl_regional[f"T{T_map}"].values

idw_rfa = IDWInterpolator(power=2)
idw_rfa.fit(station_coords, x_col="lon", y_col="lat", value_col=f"T{T_map}")
rl_map = idw_rfa.predict(grid2, x_col="lon", y_col="lat")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
im = ax.pcolormesh(GLON2, GLAT2, rl_map.reshape(GLAT2.shape),
                   cmap="YlOrRd", shading="auto")
ax.scatter(station_coords.lon, station_coords.lat,
           c=station_coords[f"T{T_map}"], cmap="YlOrRd",
           edgecolors="k", s=80, zorder=3, linewidths=0.8)
for i, name in enumerate(station_names):
    ax.annotate(name, (station_coords.lon.iloc[i], station_coords.lat.iloc[i]),
                textcoords="offset points", xytext=(5, 3), fontsize=7)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label=f"Q_T{T_map} (m³/s)")
ax.set_xlabel("Longitude")
ax.set_ylabel("Latitude")
ax.set_title(f"Regional T={T_map}-year flood frequency map", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

    

    from pyhydra.climate.spatial_analysis import IDWInterpolator

# Approximate station coordinates (Iberian Peninsula)
station_coords = pd.DataFrame({
    "lon": [-0.88, -5.63, -5.10, -5.60, -0.39, -1.05, -7.86, -1.16],
    "lat": [41.66, 39.68, 41.55, 37.95, 39.46, 38.11, 42.60, 39.17],
}, index=station_names)

# Grid for Spain
glon2 = np.arange(-9.5, 4.5, 0.3)
glat2 = np.arange(35.5, 44.5, 0.3)
GLON2, GLAT2 = np.meshgrid(glon2, glat2)
grid2 = pd.DataFrame({"lon": GLON2.ravel(), "lat": GLAT2.ravel()})

T_map = 100
station_coords[f"T{T_map}"] = rl_regional[f"T{T_map}"].values

idw_rfa = IDWInterpolator(power=2)
idw_rfa.fit(station_coords, x_col="lon", y_col="lat", value_col=f"T{T_map}")
rl_map = idw_rfa.predict(grid2, x_col="lon", y_col="lat")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
im = ax.pcolormesh(GLON2, GLAT2, rl_map.reshape(GLAT2.shape),
                   cmap="YlOrRd", shading="auto")
ax.scatter(station_coords.lon, station_coords.lat,
           c=station_coords[f"T{T_map}"], cmap="YlOrRd",
           edgecolors="k", s=80, zorder=3, linewidths=0.8)
for i, name in enumerate(station_names):
    ax.annotate(name, (station_coords.lon.iloc[i], station_coords.lat.iloc[i]),
                textcoords="offset points", xytext=(5, 3), fontsize=7)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label=f"Q_T{T_map} (m³/s)")
ax.set_xlabel("Longitude")
ax.set_ylabel("Latitude")
ax.set_title(f"Regional T={T_map}-year flood frequency map", fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    


    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    

    ✅ Next steps¶
    Regional to site workflow summary¶
    Observed annual maxima at 8+ stations
    ↓
1. Check homogeneity: L-CV CoV < 0.15  →  OK to pool
   (If not: split region or use hierarchical Bayes)
    ↓
2. Compute index floods (station means)
    ↓
3. Fit regional GEV to pooled normalised series (L-moments preferred)
    ↓
4. Site-specific T-year quantile = regional_q(T) × index_flood(site)
    ↓
5. Validate with LOO cross-validation

    

    When to prefer each method¶
    


    
Method
Best scenario

    
    


    
Regional L-moments
Short records (n < 30), homogeneous region

    

    
At-site MLE
Long records (n > 40), unique local regime

    

    
Hierarchical Bayes
Mixed record lengths, heterogeneous region (see bayes_hierarchical)

    
    

    

    Links to downstream analyses¶
      

    • Flood frequency map → interpolation notebook (IDW/Kriging of T-year quantiles)
      • 

    • Compound analysis → use T-year discharge and precipitation jointly → compound_flooding notebook
      • 

    • Design event → use the T=100 return level as design discharge for HEC-HMS or SWAT calibration
      •

      Material for MkDocs
